query: pathophysiology of spinal subdural hematoma and its relation to intracranial aneurysm rupture

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hypotension -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several causes of spontaneous ICH have been hypothesized. The most widely accepted idea amongst the scientific community is the mechanism of a CSF leak. This primary theory describes CSF leak by disruption of the meninges in the spine. Minor trauma, such as twist or stretch of the meninges, has been found in many cases to cause rupture of underlying spinal epidural cysts or tear in the dural nerve sheath. Dural tear has been identified as the second most common cause of CSF leak, while leaks from meningeal diverticula represent the leading cause. Notably, several studies have found the latter to be the case in patients with underlying connective tissue disorders, such as Marfan's syndrome, due to predisposition for dural diverticula. [1] [5] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cerebral Spinal Fluid -- Clinical Significance -- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: SAH refers to bleeding into the subarachnoid space, where blood mixes with CSF. Traumatic injury is the leading cause of SAH. Among nontraumatic cases, approximately 80% result from rupture of a cerebral aneurysm. Other nontraumatic etiologies include arteriovenous malformations and vasculitic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cranial Meninges -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatic and atraumatic intracranial hemorrhages within different locations of the meninges are a common set of conditions that are directly related to the structure and function of the meninges and can be classified by their specific location within the meningeal layers. For example, an epidural hematoma is the collection of blood in the tight potential space between the dura and the skull (i.e., the epidural space). [15] This condition is usually caused by the rupturing of the middle meningeal artery as a consequence of the fracture of the temporal bone as a result of head trauma. As the hemorrhage rapidly expands under arterial pressure, the dura peels away from the calvarium, creating a lens-shaped biconvex hematoma, as seen by a CT scan. As the hematoma compresses the underlying brain tissue, brain herniation can occur and lead to death unless treated with surgery. Other intracranial hemorrhages include subdural hematomas, which usually result from rupturing of the bridging veins and the accumulation of blood in the potential subdural space between the dura and the arachnoid, [16] and subarachnoid hemorrhages, which occur in the subarachnoid space. [17] Subarachnoid hemorrhages are often further classified as nontraumatic (spontaneous) or traumatic; nontraumatic subarachnoid hemorrhages are usually a result of a spontaneous aneurysm (or rupture) of an artery in the space between the arachnoid and the pia and present with a sudden onset headache. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Intracranial Hypotension -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A CSF leak can be congenital or traumatic due to dural defects. These defects are usually present in the form of fistulas or tears, which result in CSF leaks. Spontaneous intracranial hypotension is actually due to a decrease in volume of the CSF instead of the old notion that SIH is due to a barometric drop in CSF pressure. [3] There are various factors that can lead to leakage of CSF, like the absence of dura around the nerve root sheaths, congenital connective tissue disorders causing structural abnormalities, osteophyte protrusions, and the herniation of the spinal disc. Focal weakness in the dura can be found around the thoracic and lumbar spine. These arachnoid diverticula are especially prone to CSF leaks. [4] [5] [6] Trauma, surgery, and over drainage of the CSF shunt can also cause spontaneous intracranial hypotension. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cerebral Spinal Fluid -- Clinical Significance -- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: Noncontrast CT imaging of the head serves as the initial diagnostic modality. Sensitivity is highest within the first few hours after hemorrhage but declines over time. Lumbar puncture is indicated in patients with an unremarkable head scan but continued suspicion for SAH. A positive result includes persistent red blood cells in both the 1st and 4th collection tubes or the presence of xanthochromia. Management focuses on preventing rebleeding and minimizing secondary brain injury through careful hemodynamic monitoring, neurosurgical intervention, and supportive care. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hypertension -- Etiology -- Primary or Intracranial Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Trauma, producing epidural hematoma, subdural hematoma, intracerebral hemorrhage, subarachnoid hemorrhage, or contusions Brain tumors Ischemic stroke Nontraumatic intracerebral hemorrhage from aneurysm rupture or hypertensive hemorrhage Idiopathic or benign intracranial hypertension Hydrocephalus Meningitis Congenital malformations, including aqueductal stenosis, Dandy-Walker malformation, and Chiari malformation

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebrospinal Fluid -- Clinical Significance -- Spontaneous Intracranial Hypotension
retrieved_docs.content: Prompt brain and spinal MRI should be obtained early in the course of symptoms to detect extradural fluid collections. Brain MRI with contrast often reveals diffuse meningeal enhancement, engorged venous sinuses, and brain sagging. Spinal MRI may demonstrate longitudinal epidural collections, which are typically caused by discogenic microspurs or dural tears. Patient positioning for myelography depends on the anatomic extent of the fluid collection.

retrieved_docs.title: Brain Trauma (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: If there is a known injury, the differential includes concussion, intracranial hemorrhage, and diffuse axonal injury. Classically, an epidural hematoma occurs from the tearing of the middle meningeal artery and results in a convex-shaped hematoma between the skull and dura mater; the blood does not cross the suture lines. A subdural hematoma classically occurs due to the rupture of the bridging veins and results in a crescent-shaped hemorrhage between the dura mater and the arachnoid mater. There may also be intraparenchymal hemorrhage or intraventricular hemorrhage. In contrast to subarachnoid hemorrhage due to aneurysm rupture in which blood tends to collect around the circle of Willis, traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage typically occurs near skull fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Head Trauma -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhage may occur in multiple compartments outside and within the brain, including subarachnoid hemorrhage, subdural hematoma, epidural hematoma, contusions within the brain parenchyma itself, and cephalohematoma (birth-related injury) where a hematoma exists in the compartment between the periosteum and underlying calvarium. [11] Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) is present to some degree in the majority of patients with moderate-to-severe TBI. [12] DAI is typically caused by a rapid rotational or deceleration force that causes stretching and tearing of neurons, leading to focal areas of hemorrhage that are not always detected on the initial CT (computed tomography) scan but later identified on magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). DAI is a subtype of TBI that can present with intractable coma without elevated ICP.

retrieved_docs.title: Traumatic Brain Injury (Archive) -- Pathophysiology -- Extra-axial Hematoma
retrieved_docs.content: Extra-axial hematomas include both epidural hematomas (EDH) and subdural hematomas (SDH). EDH usually results from bleeding from the middle meningeal artery and its branches or a fracture and is usually acute. [7] SDH can result from the bleeding of a bridging vein and can be acute or chronic.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebrospinal Fluid -- Clinical Significance -- Spontaneous Intracranial Hypotension
retrieved_docs.content: Spontaneous intracranial hypotension results from spinal CSF leakage due to dural tears, diverticula, or CSF-venous fistula. This condition affects approximately 4 per 100,000 individuals annually. Most patients develop orthostatic headache, which begins or worsens upon standing and improves with recumbency.

retrieved_docs.title: Intracranial Hemorrhage -- Etiology -- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
retrieved_docs.content: A subarachnoid hemorrhage refers to bleeding into the subarachnoid space. Subarachnoid hemorrhage is divided into traumatic versus non-traumatic subarachnoid hemorrhage. A second categorization divides subarachnoid hemorrhage into an aneurysmal and non-aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage. Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage occurs after the rupture of a cerebral aneurysm. Non-aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage is bleeding into the subarachnoid space without identifiable aneurysms. Non-aneurysmal subarachnoid bleeding most commonly occurs after trauma with a blunt head injury with or without penetrating trauma or sudden acceleration changes to the head. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Trigonocephaly -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical complications after metopic craniosynostosis correction are usually relatively few and minor. [37] [57] [76] These include: Excessive blood loss Dural tears Meningitis Stroke Pseudomeningoceles Hyperthermia Hypoactivity Local wound infection Epidural abscess Subcutaneous hematoma Epidural hematoma Subdural hematoma Air embolus Incomplete correction Temporal hollowing Stretched scar/keloid Reoperation Contact dermatitis from helmet use Scalp abrasion from helmet use

retrieved_docs.title: Assessment and Management of Traumatic Injuries in Older Adults -- Pathophysiology -- Neurologic
retrieved_docs.content: The brain parenchyma also atrophies and loses volume with age, leading to stretching of the bridging dural veins. When an older individual has a sudden inertial change, such as a fall with a head strike, these veins are at high risk for rupture, leading to subdural hemorrhage. Compounded with the fact that the brain shrinks in size, but the calvarium maintains a fixed volume, clinical changes associated with intracranial hypertension often present in a delayed fashion as it takes more blood volume to lead to brain compression and shift. This process is somewhat protective because older patients may not immediately have significant clinical deterioration or need emergent surgical intervention to evacuate the hemorrhage.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Stroke -- Pathophysiology -- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH)
retrieved_docs.content: When an aneurysm ruptures, arterial blood is released into the subarachnoid space, rapidly disseminating through the cerebrospinal fluid and increasing ICP. The arterial blood can also extend into the intraventricular space and brain parenchyma. Secondary brain injury can occur due to several factors, including ICH, IVH, ICP, hydrocephalus, subdural hematoma, or delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI). [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebrospinal Fluid -- Clinical Significance -- Spontaneous Intracranial Hypotension
retrieved_docs.content: CT myelography, digital subtraction myelography, and ultrafast CT myelography are highly sensitive for detecting leaks in this setting. A CSF-venous fistula is suspected when no epidural collection is seen on MRI. In these cases, CT myelography and digital subtraction myelography performed in the lateral decubitus position improve visualization of the fistula.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1011
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is classified as an إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury) and is associated with an إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are identified as types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, indicating that skull‑base fractures are encompassed within the broader category of intracranial injuries. Additionally, إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is recognized as a subtype of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, further linking closed traumatic brain events to the same injury classification. Together, these relationships illustrate that various traumatic brain conditions—including subarachnoid hemorrhage from an open wound, anterior and middle fossa fractures, and closed head trauma—are grouped under the umbrella of intracranial injury, underscoring their interconnected nature for diagnosis, coding, and patient management.

Neurological trauma is illustrated by “Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness”, which is associated with internal injury (إصابة داخلية) and, like sports, arm, and back injuries, belongs to the SMQ hierarchy. Overall, the taxonomy integrates transport and non‑transport mechanisms, anatomical injury locations, intentional versus unintentional intent, and temporal/contextual factors, providing a comprehensive framework for classifying, querying, and managing the full spectrum of traumatic presentations in clinical practice.

Additionally, subarachnoid hemorrhage with an open intracranial wound is associated with internal injury (إصابة داخلية), linking specific intracranial bleeding to broader internal traumatic damage. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 83
retrieved_docs.content: Antemortem injury is mapped to an Unspecified site injury, which serves as a parent node for the category “Circumstances Related to Injury or Death” and is broadly linked to intracranial injury (Arabic “إصابة داخل الجمجمة”). Within the injury taxonomy, “Head and neck injury” (Arabic “إصابة داخل الجمجمة”) is a broader concept that includes cerebral concussion (“الارتجاج المخي”), wound (“جرح”), and subcategories such as subarachnoid hemorrhage following an open intracranial wound with unspecified consciousness. Specific fracture entities—Fracture of anterior fossa, Fracture of middle fossa, and closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration/contusion—are instances of “إصابة داخل الجمجمة” and have dual membership in the skull‑fracture group and the intracranial injury domain. “Fracture of a cervical vertebra” (كسر في الفقرة الرقبية) is classified under “other fractures,” is a child of neck injury (“إصابة في الرقبة”), and frequently co‑occurs with pelvic fracture (كسر الحوض).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2765
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is linked to both the etiology and the prevention and control of several medical conditions. It is associated with the etiology and with prevention and control of premature birth (ولادة قبل الأوان), tear (تمزق), death (التموت), cystic urinary disease (بيلة سِّيسْتينِية), and subdural hemorrhage (نزيف دموي تحت الجافية). In addition, acquired foot deformities are also connected to the etiology and to prevention and control, indicating that these deformities have identifiable causes and can be addressed through preventive measures. This integrated perspective highlights the importance of understanding underlying causes and implementing preventive strategies across a range of conditions, from obstetric complications and traumatic injuries to neurological hemorrhages and musculoskeletal deformities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2459
retrieved_docs.content: Fracture of anterior fossa and fracture of middle fossa are both classified as إِصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). Each represents a specific type of skull‑base fracture, with the anterior fossa fracture involving the front portion of the cranial base and the middle fossa fracture affecting the central region of the cranial base. Both conditions fall under the broader category of injuries inside the skull, highlighting their shared nature as intracranial traumatic lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 798
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness is classified as an إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury) and is associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Both Fracture of anterior fossa and Fracture of middle fossa are specific types of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, illustrating that fractures of the anterior and middle cranial fossae fall under the broader category of intracranial injury. The entity إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) is also defined as a subtype of إصابة داخل الجمجمة, indicating that closed‑head trauma is encompassed within the same injury classification. Additionally, the term hlava has a relationship with إصابة داخل الجمجمة, further linking it to intracranial injury concepts. Together, these relationships demonstrate how various forms of head trauma—ranging from subarachnoid hemorrhage with open wounds to specific cranial fossa fractures and closed‑head injuries—are organized under the unified concept of intracranial injury, which is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and coordinated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: Subarachnoid hemorrhage following injury with open intracranial wound, unspecified state of consciousness, fractures of the anterior fossa and middle fossa, and Traumatisch hersenoedeem (traumatic brain edema) are all categorized as إصابة داخل الجمجمة (intracranial injury). The subarachnoid hemorrhage is additionally associated with إصابة داخلية (internal injury). Traumatisch hersenoedeem is identified as a specific form of أوديما دماغية (brain edema), indicating that traumatic brain edema is a subtype of general brain edema while still belonging to the broader class of intracranial injuries. The relationship “isa” linking إصابة الرأس المغلقة (closed head injury) to إصابة داخل الجمجمة further clarifies that closed head injuries are also considered intracranial injuries. Together, these hierarchical and associative links define a structured understanding of various hemorrhagic, fracture‑related, and edematous conditions as subsets of intracranial trauma, highlighting their interconnections for clinical classification and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2464
retrieved_docs.content: The condition “Closed fracture of vault of skull with cerebral laceration AND/OR contusion” is a specific subtype of “كسر في الجمجمة.” It denotes a closed fracture involving the cranial vault that is accompanied by a laceration or contusion of the brain tissue, and it is classified under the broader category of skull fractures. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians accurately categorize the injury, anticipate associated intracranial damage, and guide appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Unspecified site injury is a broad injury category that encompasses more specific conditions such as intracranial injury, indicating that injuries without a clearly defined location may include trauma to the brain. This category is primarily derived from the concept of multiple injuries (إصابات متعددة), reflecting that when several injuries occur together or the exact site is not specified, they are often grouped under a general “unspecified site” label. Recognizing that unspecified site injury can represent both intracranial trauma and a collection of multiple injuries helps clinicians adopt a comprehensive assessment approach, ensuring that potential brain involvement is not overlooked and that the overall injury burden is appropriately addressed in patient care.
